channel.title: Fetch on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Fetch on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Git Fetch Failed
item.description:
What This Error Means

A Git fetch error means the local repository could not download commits, refs, or objects from the remote repository. This is typically a connectivity or authentication issue between your machine and the remote server.

Common Causes

  • Remote URL is incorrect or the repository was deleted
  • SSH key is not configured or authentication failed
  • Network connectivity issues or firewall blocking access
  • Remote repository requires two-factor authentication
  • SSL certificate verification failure
  • Repository size exceeds server limits

How to Fix

Verify Remote Configuration

git remote -v
git remote get-url origin

Test Remote Connection

ssh -T git@github.com
# For GitHub:
# Hi username! You've successfully authenticated

Update Remote URL

git remote set-url origin <new-url>

Fetch with Verbose Output

git fetch --verbose

Fetch All Remotes

git fetch --all

Fix SSL Issues

git config --global http.sslVerify false # Temporary only
# Better: update CA certificates

Examples

# Example 1: Basic fetch
git fetch origin
# fatal: unable to access 'https://...'
# Fix: check URL, credentials

# Example 2: Fetch specific branch
git fetch origin main

# Example 3: Fetch with prune (remove stale remote branches)
git fetch --prune

# Example 4: Fetch and merge
git pull --ff-only origin main
# If this fails, try:
git fetch origin
git merge origin/main

Related Errors

  • Git Push Error — push rejected
  • Git LFS Error — LFS pointer mismatch
  • Git Submodule Error — submodule update failed

item.title: [Solution] Git Shallow Clone Error — fetch depth insufficient
item.description:
Git Shallow Clone Error — fetch depth insufficient

Shallow clones contain only a limited number of commits. Errors occur when operations require history that wasn’t fetched, such as rebasing, blame, or finding merge bases.

Common Causes

  • Cloned with --depth=1 which only fetches the latest commit
  • CI/CD pipelines use shallow clones for speed
  • Operations like git blame need full history
  • Merge or rebase requires common ancestor not in shallow history

How to Fix

Fetch Full History

git fetch --unshallow

Fetch More History

git fetch --deepen=100

Clone with Full History

git clone <repo-url>

Clone with Specific Depth

git clone --depth=50 <repo-url>

Check Shallow Status

git rev-parse --is-shallow-repository

Examples

# Example 1: Blame fails on shallow clone
git blame src/app.js
# fatal: cannot do a partial blame during a squash merge
# Fix: git fetch --unshallow

# Example 2: Rebase fails
git rebase main
# error: could not find common ancestor
# Fix: git fetch --unshallow && git rebase main

# Example 3: Check if repository is shallow
git rev-parse --is-shallow-repository
# true

Related Errors

  • Submodule Error — submodule not initialized
  • Branch Not Found — branch does not exist

item.title: [Solution] JavaScript TypeError: Failed to fetch — Network Error Fix
item.description:
TypeError: Failed to fetch

A TypeError: Failed to fetch is thrown when the fetch() API cannot complete a network request. The error is intentionally vague for security reasons — it doesn’t reveal whether the failure was due to network issues, CORS, DNS resolution, or server errors. The actual cause must be investigated through other means.

Description

Failed to fetch is a blanket error that covers many network-level failures. Browsers intentionally hide the real reason to prevent information leakage. Common underlying causes:

item.title: [Solution] node-fetch Invalid Response Fix
item.description:
node-fetch Invalid Response

Fix node-fetch errors. Handle network failures, invalid responses, and request configuration..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access issues

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing dependencies
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and dependencies

// Check configuration values and ensure required dependencies are installed
// Verify the module/package is properly configured for your environment

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

try {
 // Use the module/package with proper error handling
} catch (err) {
 console.error('Error:', err.message);
 // Handle gracefully
}

Fix 3: Add retry and timeout logic

// For network operations, add timeout and retry logic
// For file operations, check existence before accessing

Examples

// Common error handling pattern
try {
 // Operation that may fail
} catch (err) {
 if (err.code === 'ECONNREFUSED') {
 console.error('Connection refused - check if service is running');
 } else if (err.code === 'ETIMEDOUT') {
 console.error('Operation timed out');
 }
}

Related Errors

  • ECONNREFUSED — connection refused
  • ETIMEDOUT — timeout
  • ENOENT — file not found

item.title: [Solution] Swift CoreData Fetch Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A CoreData fetch error occurs when a fetch request fails. This can happen due to incorrect predicates, missing entities, or fetch request configuration issues.

Common Causes

  • Invalid predicate format
  • Entity name mismatch
  • Missing sort descriptors for batch faulting
  • Fetch limit exceeded

How to Fix

// WRONG: Invalid predicate
let request = NSFetchRequest<User>(entityName: "User")
request.predicate = NSPredicate(format: "name == %@", argumentArray: nil) // Wrong format

// CORRECT: Valid predicate
let request = NSFetchRequest<User>(entityName: "User")
request.predicate = NSPredicate(format: "name == %@", "Alice")

// WRONG: Entity name typo
let request = NSFetchRequest<User>(entityName: "User") // Entity is "Users"

// CORRECT: Match entity name exactly
let request = NSFetchRequest<User>(entityName: "Users")

// WRONG: Not handling fetch errors
let users = try viewContext.fetch(request) // May throw

// CORRECT: Handle fetch errors
do {
 let users = try viewContext.fetch(request)
} catch {
 print("Fetch failed: \(error)")
}

Examples

// Example 1: Basic fetch
let request: NSFetchRequest<User> = User.fetchRequest()
let users = try viewContext.fetch(request)

// Example 2: Fetch with predicate
let request: NSFetchRequest<User> = User.fetchRequest()
request.predicate = NSPredicate(format: "age > %d", 18)
let adults = try viewContext.fetch(request)

// Example 3: Fetch with sort
let request: NSFetchRequest<User> = User.fetchRequest()
request.sortDescriptors = [NSSortDescriptor(key: "name", ascending: true)]
let sortedUsers = try viewContext.fetch(request)

Related Errors

  • CoreData persistence error — persistence issues
  • CoreData save error — save failed
  • CoreData validation error — validation failed

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2820 — Action Is Not a Recognized Action
item.description:
TS2820 — Action ‘X’ is not a recognized action

TS2820 occurs when you use an unrecognized action property in a RequestInit or similar fetch-related configuration. The action property expects specific HTTP method strings, and TypeScript validates that the value is one of the recognized methods.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in HTTP method
fetch("/api", { action: "get" }); // TS2820: 'get' is not a recognized action

// Cause 2: Using lowercase instead of uppercase
fetch("/api", { method: "get" }); // TS2820 if type expects uppercase

// Cause 3: Custom action string not in the union
fetch("/api", { action: "CUSTOM_ACTION" }); // TS2820

// Cause 4: Wrong property name
fetch("/api", { action: "GET" }); // TS2820 if 'action' isn't a valid property

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct HTTP method

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { action: "get" });

// Correct — use 'method' instead of 'action'
fetch("/api", { method: "GET" });

Fix 2: Use valid HTTP methods

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { method: "get" }); // lowercase

// Correct — uppercase
fetch("/api", { method: "GET" });

Fix 3: Use the correct RequestInit property

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { action: "POST" });

// Correct
fetch("/api", { method: "POST" });

Fix 4: Type the request options properly

const options: RequestInit = {
 method: "GET",
 headers: {
 "Content-Type": "application/json",
 },
};
fetch("/api", options);

Examples

// Example 1: Confusing 'action' with 'method'
fetch("/api/users", {
 action: "POST", // TS2820
 body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Alice" }),
});

// Fix:
fetch("/api/users", {
 method: "POST",
 body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Alice" }),
});

// Example 2: GraphQL fetch with custom options
fetch("/graphql", {
 method: "POST", // Not 'action'
 headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
 body: JSON.stringify({ query: "{ user { name } }" }),
});

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — using wrong property name.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — wrong type in function args.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — type mismatch.

item.title: Network - fetch timeout in React Native
item.description:
A network timeout error in React Native occurs when a fetch or XMLHttpRequest does not receive a response within the allowed time. This can happen on both platforms and may be triggered by slow servers, network issues, or incorrect request configuration.

Common Causes

  • Server is slow to respond or overloaded
  • No internet connectivity on the device
  • DNS resolution failure
  • Request timeout is too short for the API
  • Android cleartext traffic blocked by default
  • Large payloads without streaming

How to Fix

  1. Add timeout to fetch requests using AbortController:
const fetchWithTimeout = async (url, options = {}, timeout = 10000) => {
 const controller = new AbortController();
 const id = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), timeout);

 try {
 const response = await fetch(url, {
 ...options,
 signal: controller.signal,
 });
 clearTimeout(id);
 return response;
 } catch (error) {
 clearTimeout(id);
 if (error.name === 'AbortError') {
 throw new Error('Request timed out');
 }
 throw error;
 }
};

const data = await fetchWithTimeout('https://api.example.com/data', {}, 15000);
  1. Enable cleartext traffic on Android in android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml:
<application
 android:usesCleartextTraffic="true"
 ... >
  1. Check network connectivity before requests:
import NetInfo from '@react-native-community/netinfo';

const checkAndFetch = async (url) => {
 const state = await NetInfo.fetch();
 if (!state.isConnected) {
 throw new Error('No internet connection');
 }
 return fetch(url);
};
  1. Use retry logic for flaky connections:
const fetchWithRetry = async (url, retries = 3, delay = 1000) => {
 for (let i = 0; i < retries; i++) {
 try {
 return await fetchWithTimeout(url, {}, 10000);
 } catch (error) {
 if (i === retries - 1) throw error;
 await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, delay * (i + 1)));
 }
 }
};

Examples

// Network timeout error
fetch('https://api.example.com/data')
 .then(res => res.json())
 .catch(err => {
 // TypeError: Network request failed
 // or AbortError: Aborted
 });

Related Errors

  • Bundler error
  • RedBox error

item.title: Network request failed
item.description:
This error occurs when a network request fails in React Native due to connectivity issues, invalid URLs, or SSL certificate problems. The fetch API throws a TypeError with the message “Network request failed”.

Common Causes

  • Device has no internet connection
  • Invalid or unreachable URL (e.g. localhost on a real device)
  • SSL certificate issues (self-signed certs on Android)
  • iOS App Transport Security blocking HTTP requests

How to Fix

  1. Handle network errors in fetch calls:
async function fetchData() {
 try {
 const response = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data');
 if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${response.status}`);
 return await response.json();
 } catch (error) {
 if (error.message === 'Network request failed') {
 console.log('No internet connection');
 }
 throw error;
 }
}
  1. Allow HTTP requests in iOS (development only):
<!-- ios/YourApp/Info.plist -->
<key>NSAppTransportSecurity</key>
<dict>
 <key>NSAllowsLocalNetworking</key>
 <true/>
</dict>
  1. Allow cleartext traffic on Android:
<!-- android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml -->
<application
 android:usesCleartextTraffic="true"
 ...>
  1. Add a network status check before requests:
import NetInfo from '@react-native-community/netinfo';

const state = await NetInfo.fetch();
if (!state.isConnected) {
 Alert.alert('Offline', 'Please check your internet connection');
 return;
}

Examples

fetch('http://10.0.2.2:3000/api') // Works on Android emulator, not on real device
 .then(res => res.json())
 .catch(err => console.log(err));
// TypeError: Network request failed

Related Errors

  • Gradle/Xcode build error
